solution_html:

Ecell = 3.17 V

= 3.17 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.130}{(0.0001)^{2}}{/tex}
= 3.17 V - 0.21 V = 2.96 V

question_text: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with 0.1 mol L -1 KCl solution is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . If the resistance of the same cell when filled with 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution is 520 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution. The conductivity of 0.1 mol L -1 KCl solution is 1.29 S/m.
question_html: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with 0.1 mol L-1 KCl solution is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. If the resistance of the same cell when filled with 0.02 mol L-1 KCl solution is 520 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of 0.02 mol L-1 KCl solution. The conductivity of 0.1 mol L-1 KCl solution is 1.29 S/m.
solution_text: The cell constant is given by the equation: Cell constant = G* = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} resistance = 1.29 S/m {tex}\times{/tex} 100 Ω = 129 m -1 = 1.29 cm -1 Conductivity of 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution = cell constant / resistance = {tex}\frac{G^{*}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{129 {m}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = 0.248 S m -1 Concentration = 0.02 mol L -1 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.02 mol m -3 = 20 mol m -3 Molar conductivity = A m = {tex}\frac{K}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{248 \times 10^{-3} {Sm}^{-1}}{20 {mol\;m}^{-3}}{/tex} = 124 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 S m 2 mol -1 Alternatively, conductivity of solution K = {tex}\frac{1.29 {cm}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = = 0.248 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm -1 and molar conductivity of solution A m = K {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 cm 3 L -1 molarity -1 = {tex}\frac{0.248 \times 10^{-2} {S} {cm}^{-1} \times 1000 {cm}^{3} {L}^{-1}}{0.02 {mol} {L}^{-1}}{/tex} = 124 S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
The cell constant is given by the equation:
Cell constant = G* = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} resistance
= 1.29 S/m {tex}\times{/tex} 100 Ω = 129 m-1 = 1.29 cm-1
Conductivity of 0.02 mol L-1 KCl solution = cell constant / resistance
= {tex}\frac{G^{*}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{129 {m}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = 0.248 S m-1
Concentration = 0.02 mol L-1 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.02 mol m-3 = 20 mol m-3
Molar conductivity = Am = {tex}\frac{K}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{248 \times 10^{-3} {Sm}^{-1}}{20 {mol\;m}^{-3}}{/tex} = 124 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 S m2mol-1
Alternatively, conductivity of solution K = {tex}\frac{1.29 {cm}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = = 0.248 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 S cm-1
and molar conductivity of solution Am = K {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 cm3 L-1 molarity-1
= {tex}\frac{0.248 \times 10^{-2} {S} {cm}^{-1} \times 1000 {cm}^{3} {L}^{-1}}{0.02 {mol} {L}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 124 S cm2 mol-1

question_text: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 mol L -1 NaOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 50 cm is 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity.
question_html: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 mol L-1 NaOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 50 cm is 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity.
solution_text: Given, concentration of NaOH = 0.05M diameter = 1 cm, length = 50 cm, resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm 2 Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135 {tex}\Omega{/tex} cm Conductivity, {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
Given,
concentration of NaOH = 0.05M 
diameter = 1 cm,
length = 50 cm,
resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm2
Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135{tex}\Omega{/tex} cm
Conductivity,{tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm2 mol-1

Ion {tex}\lambda^{\circ}{/tex}/(S cm2mol-1) Ion {tex}\lambda^{\circ}{/tex}/(S cm2mol-1)

solution_text: We know from Kohlrausch law that molar conductivity of CaCl ​​​2 ​​ and MgSO ​​​4 {tex}\Lambda_{m\left({CaCl}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{Ca}^{2+}}^{\circ}+2 \lambda_{{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 119.0 S cm 2 mol -1 + 2(76.3) S cm 2 mol -1 = (119.0 + 152.6) S cm 2 mol -1 = 271.6 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(Mg_{} S O_{4}\right)}^{o}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{Mg}^{2+}}^{\circ}+ \lambda_{{SO}_{{4}}^{2-}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 106.0 S cm 2 mol -1 + 160.0 S cm 2 mol -1 = 266 S cm 2 mol -1

{tex}\Lambda_{m\left({CaCl}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{Ca}^{2+}}^{\circ}+2 \lambda_{{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 119.0 S cm2 mol-1 + 2(76.3) S cm2 mol-1

question_html: {tex}\Lambda_{m}^{0}{/tex}for NaCl, HCl and NaAc are 126.4, 425.9 and 91.0 S cm2 mol-1 respectively. Calculate {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}{/tex} for HAc.
solution_text: {tex}A_{\text {m(HAc)}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{H}^{+}}^{{o}}+\lambda_{{Ac}^{-}}^{{o}}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{H}^{+}}^{{o}}{/tex} + {tex}\lambda_{{cl}^{-}}^{{o}}{/tex} + {tex}\lambda_{{Ac}^{-}}^{{o}}{/tex} + {tex}\lambda_{{Na}^{+}}^{{o}}{/tex} - {tex}\lambda_{{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\lambda_{{Na}^{+}}^{{o}}{/tex} = {tex}\Lambda_{m({HCl})}^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\Lambda_{m({NaAc})}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\Lambda_{m({NaCl})}^{\circ}{/tex} Molar conductivity = (425.9 + 91.0 - 126.4 ) S cm 2 mol -1 = 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
{tex}A_{\text {m(HAc)}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{H}^{+}}^{{o}}+\lambda_{{Ac}^{-}}^{{o}}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{{H}^{+}}^{{o}}{/tex} + {tex}\lambda_{{cl}^{-}}^{{o}}{/tex} + {tex}\lambda_{{Ac}^{-}}^{{o}}{/tex} + {tex}\lambda_{{Na}^{+}}^{{o}}{/tex} - {tex}\lambda_{{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\lambda_{{Na}^{+}}^{{o}}{/tex}
= {tex}\Lambda_{m({HCl})}^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\Lambda_{m({NaAc})}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\Lambda_{m({NaCl})}^{\circ}{/tex}
Molar conductivity = (425.9 + 91.0 - 126.4 ) S cm2 mol-1 = 390.5 S cm2 mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How would you determine the standard electrode potential of the system Mg 2+ |Mg?
question_html: How would you determine the standard electrode potential of the system Mg2+|Mg?
solution_text: The standard electrode potential of Mg 2+ | Mg can be measured with respect to the standard hydrogen electrode, represented by H + (aq, 1 M)|Pt(s)|H 2 (g), 1 bar. A cell, consisting of Mg 2+ |Mg(ag, 1 M) as the anode and the standard hydrogen electrode H + (aq, 1 M)|Pt(s)|H 2 (g), 1 bar as the cathode. The cell represented as Mg|mg(aq, 1 M)||H + (aq, 1 M)|Pt(s)|H 2 (g), 1 bar Thus, emf of cell, {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {right }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {left }}^{\circ}{/tex} The potential of standard hydrogen electrode is always zero. Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {right }}^\circ=o{/tex} Hence, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^\circ=-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Mg}}^\circ{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Mg}}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}
solution_html:
The standard electrode potential of Mg2+ | Mg can be measured with respect to the standard hydrogen electrode, represented by H+(aq, 1 M)|Pt(s)|H2(g), 1 bar.
A cell, consisting of Mg2+ |Mg(ag, 1 M) as the anode and the standard hydrogen electrode H+(aq, 1 M)|Pt(s)|H2(g), 1 bar as the cathode.
The cell represented as
Mg|mg(aq, 1 M)||H+(aq, 1 M)|Pt(s)|H2(g), 1 bar
Thus, emf of cell, {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {right }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {left }}^{\circ}{/tex}
The potential of standard hydrogen electrode is always zero.
Therefore,
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {right }}^\circ=o{/tex}
Hence, 
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^\circ=-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Mg}}^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Mg}}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Can you store copper sulphate solutions in a zinc pot?
question_html: Can you store copper sulphate solutions in a zinc pot?
solution_text: Zinc is more reactive than copper. Therefore, zinc can displace copper from its salt solution. If copper sulphate solution is stored in a zinc pot, then zinc will displace copper from the copper sulphate solution. {tex}Zn + CuS{O_4} \to ZnS{O_4} + Cu{/tex} Hence, copper sulphate solution cannot be stored in a zinc pot.
solution_html:
Zinc is more reactive than copper. Therefore, zinc can displace copper from its salt solution. If copper sulphate solution is stored in a zinc pot, then zinc will displace copper from the copper sulphate solution.
{tex}Zn + CuS{O_4} \to ZnS{O_4} + Cu{/tex}
Hence, copper sulphate solution cannot be stored in a zinc pot.

[Ag+] = 0.002 M
[Ni2+] = 0.160 M
n = 2
According to Nernst equation:

question_text: The cell in which the following reaction occurs: {tex}2F{e^{3 + }}(aq) + 2{I^ - }(aq) \to 2F{e^{2 + }}(aq) + {I_2}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o = 0.236\;V{/tex} at 298 K. Calculate the standard Gibbs energy and the equilibrium constant of the cell reaction
question_html:
The cell in which the following reaction occurs:
{tex}2F{e^{3 + }}(aq) + 2{I^ - }(aq) \to 2F{e^{2 + }}(aq) + {I_2}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o = 0.236\;V{/tex} at 298 K.
Calculate the standard Gibbs energy and the equilibrium constant of the cell reaction
solution_text: We have, {tex}2Fe^{3+}(aq) + 2I^-(aq) \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+}(aq) + I_2(s){/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o=0.236\;V{/tex} n = 2 moles of electrons for this cell. For free Gibbs Energy, {tex}{{\vartriangle _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0} = - {\text{nF}}{{\text{E}}^0}_{{\text{cell}}}}{/tex} {tex}{ = - 2 \times 0.236 \times 96487\;{\text{C}}\;{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}}{/tex} = - 45541J = -45.54 kJ mol -1 For equilibrium we have {tex}{\log {\text{Kc}} = \frac{{{\text{nF}}{{\text{E}}^0}_{{\text{cell}}}}}{{2.303 \times {\text{RT}}}}}{/tex} {tex}{ = \frac{{2 \times 96487 \times 0.236}}{{2.303 \times 8.31 \times 298}}}{/tex} {tex}{ = \frac{{45541.864}}{{5703.1031}} = 7.9854}{/tex} or Kc = Antilog 7.9854 or {tex}{Kc = 9.62 \times {{10}^7}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,

{tex}2Fe^{3+}(aq) + 2I^-(aq) \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+}(aq) + I_2(s){/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o=0.236\;V{/tex}

n = 2 moles of electrons for this cell.

For free Gibbs Energy,

{tex}{{\vartriangle _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0} = - {\text{nF}}{{\text{E}}^0}_{{\text{cell}}}}{/tex}
{tex}{ = - 2 \times 0.236 \times 96487\;{\text{C}}\;{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}}{/tex}
= - 45541J
= -45.54 kJ mol-1

For equilibrium we have
{tex}{\log {\text{Kc}} = \frac{{{\text{nF}}{{\text{E}}^0}_{{\text{cell}}}}}{{2.303 \times {\text{RT}}}}}{/tex}
{tex}{ = \frac{{2 \times 96487 \times 0.236}}{{2.303 \times 8.31 \times 298}}}{/tex}
{tex}{ = \frac{{45541.864}}{{5703.1031}} = 7.9854}{/tex}
or Kc = Antilog 7.9854
or {tex}{Kc = 9.62 \times {{10}^7}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Why does the conductivity of a solution decrease with dilution?
question_html: Why does the conductivity of a solution decrease with dilution?
solution_text: Conductivity of a solution is the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, the conductivity decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The molar conductivity of 0.025 mol L -1 methanoic acid is 46.15 S cm 2 mol -1 . Calculate the degree of dissociation and dissociation constant. Given {tex}{\lambda ^0}{/tex} (H + ) = 349.6 S cm 2 mol -1 and {tex}{\lambda ^0}{/tex} (HCOO - ) = 54.6 S cm 2 mol -1 .
question_html: The molar conductivity of 0.025 mol L-1 methanoic acid is 46.15 S cm2mol-1. Calculate the degree of dissociation and dissociation constant. Given {tex}{\lambda ^0}{/tex}(H+) = 349.6 S cm2mol-1and {tex}{\lambda ^0}{/tex} (HCOO-) = 54.6 S cm2mol-1.
solution_text: The molar conductivity of methanoic acid {tex}\lambda _m^c = 46.1\,S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda _m^c = \left( {HCOOH} \right) = {\lambda ^0}\left( {{H^ + }} \right) + {\lambda ^0}\left( {HCO{O^ - }} \right){/tex} = 349.6 + 54.6S cm 2 mol -1 = 404.2 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha = \frac{{\lambda {m^c}}}{{\lambda {m^0}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{46.1}}{{404.2}} = 0.114{/tex} {tex}HCOOH \rightleftharpoons HCO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} Initial conc. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}} \hfill \\ n(1 - \alpha ) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} 0 \hfill \\ n\alpha \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array} {/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{x{\alpha ^2}}}{{1 - \alpha }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.025 \times {{\left( {0.114} \right)}^2}}}{{1 - 0.114}}{/tex} {tex} = 3.67 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The molar conductivity of methanoic acid
{tex}\lambda _m^c = 46.1\,S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda _m^c = \left( {HCOOH} \right) = {\lambda ^0}\left( {{H^ + }} \right) + {\lambda ^0}\left( {HCO{O^ - }} \right){/tex}
= 349.6 + 54.6S cm2 mol-1
= 404.2 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\alpha = \frac{{\lambda {m^c}}}{{\lambda {m^0}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{46.1}}{{404.2}} = 0.114{/tex}
{tex}HCOOH \rightleftharpoons HCO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex}
Initial conc. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}} \hfill \\ n(1 - \alpha ) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} 0 \hfill \\ n\alpha \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{x{\alpha ^2}}}{{1 - \alpha }}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.025 \times {{\left( {0.114} \right)}^2}}}{{1 - 0.114}}{/tex}
{tex} = 3.67 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the chemistry of recharging the lead storage battery, highlighting all the materials that are involved during recharging.
question_html: Write the chemistry of recharging the lead storage battery, highlighting all the materials that are involved during recharging.
solution_text: A lead storage battery consists of a lead anode, a grid of lead packed with lead dioxide (PbO 2 ) as cathode and a 38% solution of sulphuric acid (H 2 SO 4 ) as an electrolyte. When the battery is in use, the following cell reactions take place: At anode: {tex}Pb(s) + SO_4^{2 - }(aq) \to PbS{O_4}(s) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At cathode: {tex}PbS{O_4}(s) + SO_4^{2 - }(aq) + 4{H^ + }(aq) + 2{e^ - } \to PbS{O_4}(s) + 2{H_2}O(l){/tex} The overall cell reaction is given by, Pb(s) + PbO 2 (s)+2H 2 SO 4 (aq) {tex}\to{/tex} 2PbSO 4 (s) + 2H 2 O(l) When battery is charging, the reverse of all these reactions takes place. Hence, on charging, PbSO 4 (s) present at the anode and cathode is converted into Pb and PbO 2 , respectively. Sulphuric acid (H 2 SO 4 ) reconstitute in the reaction. The reaction may written as: {tex}2PbO_2(s)+2H_2O(l)\xrightarrow{charging}Pb(s)+PbO_2\;(s)+2H_2SO_4{/tex}
solution_html:
A lead storage battery consists of a lead anode, a grid of lead packed with lead dioxide (PbO2) as cathode and a 38% solution of sulphuric acid (H2SO4) as an electrolyte.

When the battery is in use, the following cell reactions take place:
At anode: {tex}Pb(s) + SO_4^{2 - }(aq) \to PbS{O_4}(s) + 2{e^ - }{/tex}
At cathode: {tex}PbS{O_4}(s) + SO_4^{2 - }(aq) + 4{H^ + }(aq) + 2{e^ - } \to PbS{O_4}(s) + 2{H_2}O(l){/tex}
The overall cell reaction is given by,
Pb(s) + PbO2(s)+2H2SO4(aq) {tex}\to{/tex} 2PbSO4(s) + 2H2O(l)
When battery is charging, the reverse of all these reactions takes place.
Hence, on charging, PbSO4(s) present at the anode and cathode is converted into Pb and PbO2, respectively. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) reconstitute in the reaction. The reaction may written as:
{tex}2PbO_2(s)+2H_2O(l)\xrightarrow{charging}Pb(s)+PbO_2\;(s)+2H_2SO_4{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain how rusting of iron is envisaged as setting up of an electrochemical cell.
question_html: Explain how rusting of iron is envisaged as setting up of an electrochemical cell.
solution_text: The water layer present on the surface of iron (especially in the rainy season) dissolves acidic oxides of air like CO 2 ,SO 2 etc. to from acids which dissociate to give H + ions: H 2 O + CO 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H 2 CO 3 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H + + {tex}C O_{3}^{2-}{/tex} In the presence of H + ions iron starts losing electrons at some spot to form ferrous ions, i.e. its oxidation takes place. Hence, this spot acts as the anode: Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + 2e - The electrons this released through the metal to reach another spot where H + ions and the dissolved oxygen takes up these electrons and reduction reaction takes place. Hence, this spot acts as the cathode: O 2 (g) + 4H + (aq) + 4e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H 2 O(l) The overall reaction is : 2Fe(s) + O 2 (g) + AH + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe 2+ (aq) + 2H 2 O(l) Ferrous ions are further oxidized by the atmospheric oxygen to ferric ions which combine with water molecules to form hydrated ferric oxide, Fe 2 O 3 .xH 2 O which is rust.
solution_html:
The water layer present on the surface of iron (especially in the rainy season) dissolves acidic oxides of air like CO2 ,SO2 etc. to from acids which dissociate to give H+ ions:
H2O + CO2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2CO3 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H+ + {tex}C O_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
In the presence of H+ ions iron starts losing electrons at some spot to form ferrous ions, i.e. its oxidation takes place. Hence, this spot acts as the anode:
Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2+(aq) + 2e-
The electrons this released through the metal to reach another spot where H+ ions and the dissolved oxygen takes up these electrons and reduction reaction takes place. Hence, this spot acts as the cathode:
O2(g) + 4H+(aq) + 4e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) 
The overall reaction is :
2Fe(s) + O2(g) + AH+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe2+(aq) + 2H2O(l)
Ferrous ions are further oxidized by the atmospheric oxygen to ferric ions which combine with water molecules to form hydrated ferric oxide, Fe2O3.xH2O which is rust.

question_text: Given the standard electrode potentials, K + /K = -2.93V Ag + /Ag = 0.80V Hg 2+ /Hg = 0.79V Mg 2+ /Mg = -2.37V Cr 3+ /Cr = - 0.74V Arrange these metals in their increasing order of reducing power.
question_html:

K+/K = -2.93V
Ag+/Ag = 0.80V
Hg2+/Hg = 0.79V
Mg2+/Mg = -2.37V
Cr3+/Cr = - 0.74V
Arrange these metals in their increasing order of reducing power.
solution_text: The lower the reduction potential, the higher is the reducing power. The given standard electrode potentials increase in the order of K + /K < Mg 2+ /Mg < Cr 3+ /Cr < Hg 2+ /Hg < Ag + /Ag. Hence, the reducing power of the given metals increases in the following order: Ag < Hg < Cr < Mg < K

question_text: Depict the galvanic cell in which the reaction: {tex}Zn(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} takes place. Further show: Which of the electrodes is negatively charged? The carries of current in the cell. Individual reaction at each electrode.
question_html:
Depict the galvanic cell in which the reaction:
{tex}Zn(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex}
takes place. Further show:

  1. Which of the electrodes is negatively charged?
  2. The carries of current in the cell.
  3. Individual reaction at each electrode.
solution_text: We have, {tex}Zn(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} At Cathode (Reduction): {tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow2Ag(s){/tex} At Anode (Oxidation): {tex}Zn(s)\rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq)+2e^-{/tex} The cell will be represented as: {tex}Zn(s)|Z{n^{2 + }}(aq)||A{g^ + }(aq)|Ag(s){/tex} From half cell reaction, Zinc acts as Anode, i.e. zinc electrode will be negatively charged. The current will flow from silver to zinc in the external circuit and inside the solution, ions are responsible as shown in the half cell reaction. Zn 2+ is formed in the anode container and goes to the solution and in the cathode container, Ag + goes from solution to the silver metal(cathode) and gets deposited. To maintain the concentration of ions in both the containers, salt bridge is used which contain an electrolyte i.e. KCl. At anode : {tex}Zn(s) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At cathode: {tex}A{g^ + }(aq) + e \to Ag(s){/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}Zn(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex}

At Cathode (Reduction):
{tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow2Ag(s){/tex}
At Anode (Oxidation):
{tex}Zn(s)\rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq)+2e^-{/tex}
The cell will be represented as:
{tex}Zn(s)|Z{n^{2 + }}(aq)||A{g^ + }(aq)|Ag(s){/tex}

  1. From half cell reaction, Zinc acts as Anode, i.e. zinc electrode will be negatively charged.
  2. The current will flow from silver to zinc in the external circuit and inside the solution, ions are responsible as shown in the half cell reaction. Zn2+ is formed in the anode container and goes to the solution and in the cathode container, Ag+ goes from solution to the silver metal(cathode) and gets deposited. To maintain the concentration of ions in both the containers, salt bridge is used which contain an electrolyte i.e. KCl.
  3. At anode : {tex}Zn(s) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex}
    At cathode: {tex}A{g^ + }(aq) + e \to Ag(s){/tex}

Then, {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^\Theta } = - nFE_{cell}^\Theta {/tex} = {tex} - 1 \times 96487{\rm{ }}C{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 0.03{\rm{ }}V{/tex} = -2894.61 J mol -1 = -2.89 kJ mol -1 Again, {tex} {\Delta _r}{G^\Theta } = 2.303\,RT\,In\,K{/tex} {tex}\log \,K = - \frac{{{\Delta _r}G}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 2894.61}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}}{/tex} = 0.5073 Therefore, K = antilog (0.5073) = 3.2 (approximately)

    {tex}= \frac{{ - 2894.61}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}}{/tex}
    = 0.5073
    Therefore, K = antilog (0.5073)
    = 3.2 (approximately)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define conductivity and molar conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. Discuss their variation with concentration.
question_html: Define conductivity and molar conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. Discuss their variation with concentration.
solution_text: Both conductivity and molar conductivity change with the concentration of the electrolyte. Conductivity always decreases with decrease in concentration both, for weak and strong electrolytes. This can be explained by the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution. The conductivity of a solution at any given concentration is the conductance of one unit volume of solution kept between two platinum electrodes with unit area of cross section and at a distance of unit length. This is clear from the equation: {tex}G = \frac{\kappa A}l= \kappa{/tex} (both A and l are unity in their appropriate units in m or cm) Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Since l = 1 and A = V (Volume containing 1 mole of electrolyte). Therefore, {tex}\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa A}l= \kappa V{/tex} Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration. This is because the total volume, V, of solution containing one mole of electrolyte also increases. It has been found that decrease in {tex}\kappa{/tex} on dilution of a solution is more than compensated by increase in its volume. Physically, it means that at a given concentration, {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} can be defined as the conductance of the electrolytic solution kept between the electrodes of a conductivity cell at unit distance but having area of cross section large enough to accommodate sufficient volume of solution that contains one mole of the electrolyte. When concentration approaches zero, the molar conductivity is known as limiting molar conductivity and is represented by the symbol {tex}\Lambda_m^\circ\;{/tex} . The variation in {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} with concentration is different for strong and weak electrolytes as shown in the figure. For strong electrolytes, {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation: {tex}Ʌ_m = Ʌ_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}{/tex} Weak electrolytes like acetic acid have lower degree of dissociation at higher concentrations and hence for such electrolytes, the change in {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} with dilution is due to increase in the degree of dissociation and consequently the number of ions in total volume of solution that contains 1 mol of electrolyte. In such cases {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} increases steeply on dilution, especially near lower concentrations as shown in the given figure above.

Both conductivity and molar conductivity change with the concentration of the electrolyte. Conductivity always decreases with decrease in concentration both, for weak and strong electrolytes. This can be explained by the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution. This is clear from the equation:
{tex}G = \frac{\kappa A}l= \kappa{/tex} (both A and l are unity in their appropriate units in m or cm)
Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Since l = 1 and A = V (Volume containing 1 mole of electrolyte). Therefore,
{tex}\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa A}l= \kappa V{/tex}
Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration. This is because the total volume, V, of solution containing one mole of electrolyte also increases. It has been found that decrease in {tex}\kappa{/tex} on dilution of a solution is more than compensated by increase in its volume. Physically, it means that at a given concentration, {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} can be defined as the conductance of the electrolytic solution kept between the electrodes of a conductivity cell at unit distance but having area of cross section large enough to accommodate sufficient volume of solution that contains one mole of the electrolyte. When concentration approaches zero, the molar conductivity is known as limiting molar conductivity and is represented by the symbol {tex}\Lambda_m^\circ\;{/tex}. The variation in {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} with concentration is different for strong and weak electrolytes as shown in the figure.

For strong electrolytes, {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation:
{tex}Ʌ_m = Ʌ_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}{/tex}
Weak electrolytes like acetic acid have lower degree of dissociation at higher concentrations and hence for such electrolytes, the change in {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} with dilution is due to increase in the degree of dissociation and consequently the number of ions in total volume of solution that contains 1 mol of electrolyte. In such cases {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} increases steeply on dilution, especially near lower concentrations as shown in the given figure above.

question_text: Conductivity of 0.00241 M acetic acid is 7.896 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity. If {tex}{\Lambda^o}_m{/tex} for acetic acid is 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 , what is its dissociation constant?
question_html: Conductivity of 0.00241 M acetic acid is 7.896 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1. Calculate its molar conductivity. If {tex}{\Lambda^o}_m{/tex} for acetic acid is 390.5 S cm2 mol-1, what is its dissociation constant?
solution_text: Given that, Molarity (M) = 0.00241 M Conductivity ( {tex}\kappa{/tex} ) = 7.896 x 10 -5 S cm -1 For molar conductivity, we have {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})\;\times\;1000\;(cm^3/L)}{\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7.896 \times 10^{-5}\times1000}{0.0024}{/tex} = 32.9 S cm 2 mol -1 For Dissociation Constant, we have, {tex}CH_3COOH + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CH_3OO^- + H_3O^+{/tex} M0 0 0 at t=0 M-M {tex}\alpha{/tex} +M {tex}\alpha{/tex} +M {tex}\alpha{/tex} at t=t Dissociation constant for this equation is: {tex}K_a = \frac{(M\alpha) (M\alpha)}{(M-M\alpha)}=\frac{M\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}{/tex} .......(A) Also, degree of dissociation is given by {tex}\alpha\;=\;\frac{Ʌ_m}{Ʌ_m^\circ}{/tex} ......(B) from (A) and (B), finally we have; {tex}K_a\;=\;\frac{MɅ_m^2}{Ʌ_m^օ(Ʌ_m^օ-\Lambda_m)} {/tex} After putting corresponding values, we get {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{0.00241 \times 32.9}}{{390.5(390.5 - 32.9)}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = 5.678 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex}
solution_html:

For molar conductivity, we have
{tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})\;\times\;1000\;(cm^3/L)}{\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7.896 \times 10^{-5}\times1000}{0.0024}{/tex}

question_text: How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 20.0 g of Ca from molten CaCl 2 ? 40.0 g of Al from molten Al 2 O 3 ?
question_html:
How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce

  1. 20.0 g of Ca from molten CaCl2?
  2. 40.0 g of Al from molten Al2O3?
solution_text: According to the question, {tex}C{a^{2 + }} + 2{e^{ - 1}} \to \mathop {Ca}\limits_{40\,g} {/tex} Electricity required to produce 40 g of calcium = 2 F Therefore, electricity required to produce 20 g of calcium = {tex}\frac{{2 \times 20}}{{40}}F{/tex} = 1 F According to the question, {tex}A{l^{3 + }} + 3{e^{ - 1}} \to \mathop {Al}\limits_{27g}{/tex} Electricity required to produce 27 g of Al = 3 F Therefore, electricity required to produce 40 g of Al = {tex}\frac{{3 \times 40}}{{27}}F{/tex} = 4.44 F
solution_html:

    {tex}C{a^{2 + }} + 2{e^{ - 1}} \to \mathop {Ca}\limits_{40\,g} {/tex}

    = 1 F
  2. According to the question,
    {tex}A{l^{3 + }} + 3{e^{ - 1}} \to \mathop {Al}\limits_{27g}{/tex}

    = 4.44 F

question_text: Using the standard electrode potentials given in table 3.1, predict if the reaction between the following is feasible or not: Fe 3+ (aq) and I - (aq) Ag + (aq) and Cu(s) Fe 3+ (aq) and Br - (aq) Ag(s) and Fe 3+ (aq) Br 2 (aq) and Fe 2+ (aq) Note: Standard Electrode Potentials of Fe 3+ (+0.77 V), I - (-0.54 V), Ag + (+0.80 V), Cu(-0.34 V), Br - (-1.09 V), Br 2 (+1.09 V), Ag(-0.80 V), Fe 2+ (-0.77 V)
question_html:
Using the standard electrode potentials given in table 3.1, predict if the reaction between the following is feasible or not:

  1. Fe3+(aq) and I-(aq)
  2. Ag+(aq) and Cu(s)
  3. Fe3+(aq) and Br-(aq)
  4. Ag(s) and Fe3+(aq)
  5. Br2(aq) and Fe2+(aq)

Note: Standard Electrode Potentials of Fe3+(+0.77 V), I-(-0.54 V), Ag+(+0.80 V), Cu(-0.34 V), Br-(-1.09 V), Br2(+1.09 V), Ag(-0.80 V), Fe2+(-0.77 V)
solution_text: A reaction is feasible if EMF of the cell is positive. Since E 0 for the overall reaction is positive, the reaction between Fe 3+ (aq) and I - (aq) is feasible. Since E 0 for the overall reaction is positive, the reaction between Ag + (aq) and Cu(s) is feasible. Since E 0 for the overall reaction is negative, the reaction between Fe 3+ (aq) and Br - (aq) is not feasible. Since E 0 for the overall reaction is negative, the reaction between Ag(s) and Fe 3+ (aq) is not feasible. Since E 0 for the overall reaction is positive, the reaction between Br 2 (aq) and Fe 2+ (aq) is feasible.
solution_html:
A reaction is feasible if EMF of the cell is positive.

Since E0 for the overall reaction is positive, the reaction between Fe3+(aq) and I-(aq) is feasible.

Since E0 for the overall reaction is positive, the reaction between Ag+(aq) and Cu(s) is feasible.

Since E0 for the overall reaction is negative, the reaction between Fe3+(aq) and Br-(aq) is not feasible.

Since E0 for the overall reaction is negative, the reaction between Ag(s) and Fe3+(aq) is not feasible.

Since E0 for the overall reaction is positive, the reaction between Br2(aq) and Fe2+(aq) is feasible.

question_text: Predict the products of electrolysis in each of the following: An aqueous solution of AgNO 3 with silver electrodes. An aqueous solution of AgNO 3 with platinum electrodes. A dilute solution of H 2 SO 4 with platinum electrodes. An aqueous solution of CuCl 2 with platinum electrodes.
question_html:
Predict the products of electrolysis in each of the following:

  1. An aqueous solution of AgNO3 with silver electrodes.
  2. An aqueous solution of AgNO3 with platinum electrodes.
  3. A dilute solution of H2SO4 with platinum electrodes.
  4. An aqueous solution of CuCl2 with platinum electrodes.
solution_text: At cathode: The following reduction reactions compete to take place at the cathode. {tex}Ag_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to A{g_{(s)}}{/tex} ; E o = 0.80V {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} ; E o = 0.00V The reaction with a higher value of E º takes place at the cathode. Therefore, deposition of silver will take place at the cathode. At anode: The Ag anode is attacked by NO 3 - ions. Therefore, the silver electrode at the anode dissolves in the solution to form Ag+. At cathode: The following reduction reactions compete to take place at the cathode. {tex}Ag_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to A{g_{(s)}}{/tex} ; E o = 0.80V {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} ; E o = 0.00 V The reaction with a higher value of Eº takes place at the cathode. Therefore, deposition of silver will take place at the cathode. At anode: Since Pt electrodes are inert, the anode is not attacked by NO 3 - ions. Therefore, OH - or NO 3 - ions can be oxidized at the anode. But OH - ions having a lower discharge potential and get preference and decompose to liberate O 2 . OH - → OH + e - 4OH - → 2H 2 O +O 2 At the cathode, the following reduction reaction occurs to produce H 2 gas. H + (aq) + e - → ½ H 2(g) At the anode, the following processes are possible. {tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }{/tex} ; E o = +1.23V ...(i) {tex}2SO_{4(aq)}^{2 - } \to {S_2}O_{6(aq)}^{2 - } + 2{e^ - }{/tex} ; E o = +1.96 V ....(ii) For dilute sulphuric acid, reaction (i) is preferred to produce O 2 gas. But for concentrated sulphuric acid, reaction (ii) occurs. At cathode: The following reduction reactions compete to take place at the cathode. {tex}Cu_{(aq)}^{2 + } + 2{e^ - } \to C{u_{(s)}}{/tex} ; E o = 0.34V {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} ; E o = 0.00 V The reaction with a higher value of E º takes place at the cathode. Therefore, deposition of copper will take place at the cathode. At anode: The following oxidation reactions are possible at the anode. {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^{ - 1}}{/tex} ; E o = 1.36V {tex}2{H_2}{O_{(i)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }{/tex} ; E o = +1.23V At the anode, the reaction with a lower value of E º is preferred. But due to the over-potential of oxygen, Cl - gets oxidized at the anode to produce Cl 2 gas.

1. At cathode: The following reduction reactions compete to take place at the cathode.
    {tex}Ag_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to A{g_{(s)}}{/tex}; Eo = 0.80V
    {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex}; Eo = 0.00V
